title: Reflection Loops in LangGraph: Write, Critique, Revise
description: You can build a LangGraph graph and call a model, but you haven't yet built an agent that judges its own output, revises it, and knows when to stop. This course builds that write, critique, revise loop one piece at a time on a running support-reply example, then hardens it: bounded termination so it always exits, a data rubric an LLM can actually score, LangSmith tracing that makes bugs legible, and an eval that reuses the very same rubric. Module 5 treats reflection as a budget on a cost and quality curve, and the capstone redeploys the whole stack on a brand-new domain (rewriting dense legal clauses into plain language) to prove the pattern transfers. It's TypeScript first with a Python translation track, grounded in the open-source wanderlearn-field-reporter repo, and every example runs offline with no API key.

lessons.title: Course overview: reflection is a graph, not a prompt

recall.prompt: Why build a reflection loop as a graph rather than as one longer prompt?
recall.answer: Packing draft, critique, and revise into a single prompt blurs generation and judgment so you can't see, measure, or bound them. A graph splits writing and critiquing into separate nodes joined by a cyclic edge and a stop counter, so termination, rubrics, tracing, and evals each bolt on cleanly.

recall.prompt: What does a reflection loop add that a single model call lacks?
recall.answer: A single call returns a plausible answer, which is where next-token prediction stops. The loop gives the model an explicit target and a second attempt at hitting it, and agents that critique and revise measurably beat single-pass ones.

recall.prompt: What do you need installed to run the Module 0 loop, and what can you skip?
recall.answer: You need Node 20 or newer and the cloned repo, and nothing else. There's no API key, no Docker, and no LangGraph server, because the first loop uses a deterministic stub critic and a canned writer that never touch a model or the network.

recall.prompt: What is the verifiable success signal for the loop, and why does it matter?
recall.answer: Running the test file asserts deterministically that the demo produces two revisions ending in a PASSED critique. It's the same regression signal Module 4 formalizes, so breaking the loop on purpose lets you catch a failure two modules early.

lessons.title: The Python and TypeScript translation table

recall.prompt: How does this course support Python learners without maintaining a second codebase?
recall.answer: The one runnable artifact stays TypeScript so every lesson can cite a real file, but LangGraph ships the same mental model in Python. You can spin up an optional Python environment as a Rosetta stone, and Lesson 4's translation table maps the two.

recall.prompt: Which Python LangGraph tool needs Docker and which doesn't?
recall.answer: The in-memory langgraph dev server opens LangGraph Studio to visualize a graph with no Docker required. Docker only enters with langgraph up, the self-hosted deploy path, which this course treats as out of scope.

lessons.title: Why one pass is plausible, not good

recall.prompt: Beyond naming style, what are the only two real differences between the TypeScript and Python tracks?
recall.answer: The schema library differs: TypeScript uses Zod and Python uses Pydantic, though both compile to the same JSON Schema the model sees. The async surface also differs: Python offers sync and async entrypoints while TypeScript is async throughout.

recall.prompt: Why can a Python learner follow a lesson written in TypeScript?
recall.answer: Every LangGraph concept maps one-to-one between the two languages, so the reflection loop is structurally identical and only the spelling changes. The table lets you convert any step mechanically, such as addNode to add_node or addConditionalEdges to add_conditional_edges.

lessons.title: The minimal write, critique, revise loop

recall.prompt: Why does a single-pass model reply stop at plausible instead of good?
recall.answer: Plausible is where next-token prediction ends, because the objective rewards text that sounds like a support reply, not text that resolves the customer's problem. Left ungrounded, the model produces fluent output that's confidently incomplete.

recall.prompt: What is the prompt-harder anti-pattern, and what actually fixes it?
recall.answer: Prompt-harder means piling more requirements into the generation prompt, which conflates writing with judging, has no measurable success signal, and degrades as clauses compete for attention. The fix is structural: add a separate step whose only job is to judge the first draft.

questions.prompt: The course's thesis is "reflection is a graph, not a prompt." What concretely goes wrong if you make reflection a single prompt instead?
options: The prompt grows too long to fit the model's context window
The model runs slower because it has to think twice
Generation and judgment share one forward pass, so there's no separate critique to inspect, no counter to stop on, and no node to trace
You have to pay for two API calls instead of one
questions.explanation: When writing and judging happen in one pass they compete and the quality bar loses, and you lose every seam you need later: a separate critique to inspect, a counter to bound the loop, and a node to trace. Context length isn't the problem, a single prompt is one pass not two, and it's fewer calls not more.

questions.prompt: Why does a single model call tend to return a "plausible, not good" support reply?
options: Next-token prediction optimizes for text that reads like a support reply, and nothing in that objective rewards actually resolving the issue
The model is too small to hold the whole ticket in memory
The default temperature is set too high
The model was never shown a support ticket in training
questions.explanation: Plausible is where next-token prediction stops: the model produces text shaped like a good reply, but the objective never rewards solving the customer's problem, so you get fluent, empty text (Ji et al., 2023). Model size, temperature, and training data aren't the structural cause.

questions.prompt: What is the "prompt-harder" anti-pattern, and its fix?
options: Switching to a larger model; the fix is a smaller, cheaper one
Setting temperature to zero; the fix is raising it for creativity
Retrying the same prompt until the output looks right; the fix is caching the result
Piling more requirements into the generation prompt to close the quality gap; the fix is a separate step whose only job is to judge the first
questions.explanation: Prompt-harder crams judgment into generation, so it has no measurable success signal and degrades as requirements compete for attention; the fix is a second step that judges the draft (Madaan et al., 2023). The other options change unrelated knobs, not the generation-versus-judgment split.

questions.prompt: In the minimal loop, where does the revision cap live, and where must it never live?
options: In the model's system prompt, as "revise at most three times"
In the routing function in code, as `if (revisionNumber >= MAX_REVISIONS) return END`, never in a prompt
In an environment variable read once at startup
In the LangSmith dashboard configuration
questions.explanation: The cap is a control-flow decision, so it belongs in `routeAfterCritique` as a `>=` the sampler can't override; a prompt instruction to stop is only a suggestion. An env var or dashboard setting doesn't gate the loop per pass, and putting the cap in the prompt is the exact anti-pattern.

questions.prompt: What two properties make the stub critic's checks useful, and also carry over to a good LLM critic?
options: They run fast and allocate no memory
They're written in TypeScript and validated with Zod
Each check is concretely verifiable by reading the draft, and the critic returns evidence per check
They always pass by the second revision
questions.explanation: A check you can confirm by reading the text ("names the issue", not "is empathetic") plus evidence per check forces grounded verdicts instead of rubber-stamping, and both carry to LLM-scored rubrics (Liu et al., 2023). Speed, language, and "always passing" say nothing about whether the check is a good check.

questions.prompt: Why does the loop's `history` channel use a concat reducer when the other channels are last-write-wins?
options: So every revision is appended and retained, which keeps the whole run auditable for Module 3's tracing
Because concatenating is faster than overwriting a value
Because LangGraph forces array channels to concat
So two nodes can write to history at the same time
questions.explanation: Appending every draft instead of overwriting keeps a full record of the run, which the tracing module builds on. Concat isn't chosen for speed, LangGraph lets you pick any reducer, and the motivation isn't concurrent writes.

lessons.title: Why cyclic graphs need a guaranteed exit

recall.prompt: What guarantees that the cyclic reflection graph terminates, and where does it live?
recall.answer: A max-revisions check in the routing function ends the run once the counter reaches the cap. The line if (state.revisionNumber >= MAX_REVISIONS) return END lives in code, never in a prompt, which is what makes the loop guaranteed to exit.

recall.prompt: What two properties make the stub critic useful, and why keep it deterministic in Module 0?
recall.answer: Each check is concretely verifiable by reading the text, and the critic returns evidence per check so verdicts are grounded rather than rubber-stamped. Keeping it a deterministic stub lets the whole loop run offline with a repeatable success signal, because Module 0 is about the shape of the loop, not the model.

recall.prompt: Why is a cyclic reflection loop with no code-level exit a bug rather than a feature?
recall.answer: The write node can route back to itself forever, so if the critic never passes the draft, the cycle repeats endlessly. 'The model will probably converge' is hope, not termination, and one bad input can run the loop unbounded, paying LLM latency and tokens on every wasted pass.

recall.prompt: What must be true of a cycle for it to be guaranteed to terminate?
recall.answer: Some measure has to strictly decrease toward a bound on every pass, and the loop must stop at that bound. The counter does exactly this: revisionNumber increases each pass and the router exits at MAX_REVISIONS, guaranteeing a halt no matter what the model does.

recall.prompt: In the counter pattern, which part of the graph increments the counter and which part checks it?
recall.answer: The write node increments revisionNumber by one on every pass, and the router compares it to MAX_REVISIONS and exits when the count reaches the bound. Because the counter strictly increases and the router exits at the bound, the loop halts in at most MAX_REVISIONS passes.

recall.prompt: Why does telling the model 'revise at most three times' in the prompt fail to bound the loop?
recall.answer: The model can't reliably count its own turns, a sampler or a confused completion can ignore the instruction, and you can't trace or test a bound buried in a paragraph. The cap is a control flow decision, so it belongs in the graph, not in the generator you're trying to control.

recall.prompt: What does 'converged' mean operationally, and what's the simplest honest test?
recall.answer: It means the loop's output has stopped moving. The simplest test is exact equality of the two most recent drafts after trimming whitespace; if they're identical, the writer is no longer responding to the critique and another pass would just repeat the draft and waste the same tokens.

recall.prompt: When convergence fires on a failing draft, where does the router send the run, and why not to END?
recall.answer: It routes to a human via flag_for_human, not to END. Convergence tells you the loop is done trying, not that the answer is good, so a converged but failing draft still needs a person rather than being shipped.

lessons.title: Composing the patterns in priority order

recall.prompt: What are the two honest endings of a reflection loop, and what breaks if you collapse them into a single END?
recall.answer: Resolved means the critique passed and the output is shippable; escalated means the loop stopped without passing and a human must take over. If both end at a plain END, downstream code can't tell 'shipped' from 'gave up,' so the gave-up draft gets shipped.

recall.prompt: Give one reason escalation belongs inside the graph rather than in the calling application.
recall.answer: The graph is the single source of truth for why the run stopped (passed, cap, or convergence), so re-deriving that outside duplicates the logic and invites drift. It also composes with the router, sitting beside the other exits in priority order instead of in a separate if the router can't see.

lessons.title: recursionLimit: the backstop, not the steering wheel

recall.prompt: In the composed router, what is the priority order of the four branches?
recall.answer: Success first (mark_resolved), then convergence, then the cap, then revise as the default. The checks form a top to bottom cascade, and the first matching branch wins.

recall.prompt: Why must the success check come before the cap check?
recall.answer: A draft that passes on the final allowed pass is a win, not an escalation. If the cap check ran first, that perfectly good reply would be escalated instead of shipped, so success has to dominate every other condition.

questions.prompt: Why must the revision cap be a counter in code rather than an instruction in the prompt?
options: Because prompts can't contain numbers
Because the model counts its own turns more accurately than code does
Because a code counter costs more tokens, which discourages long loops
Because a prompt instruction is a suggestion the sampler can ignore and is invisible to alerts, traces, and tests, while a `>=` in the router can't be argued with
questions.explanation: The model has no durable, trustworthy count of how many times the loop ran, a prompt cap is one confused completion from being ignored, and a bound buried in a paragraph can't be alerted on or tested; a counter in state shows up in every trace and every test. Prompts can hold numbers, the model counts worse than code, and token cost isn't the reason.

questions.prompt: Which set names three ways a reflection loop fails to terminate on its own?
options: A slow network, a rate limit, and a request timeout
An impossible rubric the writer can never satisfy, a stalled writer emitting the same draft each pass, and critic drift that flip-flops pass and fail
A missing API key, a bad prompt, and a small context window
Too many nodes, too many edges, and too much state
questions.explanation: Non-termination comes from the loop's own logic: a rubric nothing can pass, a writer stuck repeating a draft, or a nondeterministic judge that never settles. The other options are infrastructure or sizing issues, not reasons the loop can't reach an exit on its own.

questions.prompt: The max-iteration counter already bounds the loop. Why add convergence detection?
options: The counter stops a runaway but not a stall: a writer stuck on the same draft still burns the whole budget, so convergence exits early at the first repeat
Convergence detection replaces the counter so you can delete it
Convergence makes the model produce better drafts
The counter only works when a real LLM is the writer
questions.explanation: The counter caps the worst case but still pays for identical passes when the writer stalls; convergence detection notices the repeat and exits immediately, saving the wasted calls. It complements the counter rather than replacing it, doesn't improve draft quality, and the counter works with any writer.

questions.prompt: A run converges but the draft still fails the rubric. Why route it to a human instead of to a plain END?
options: Because LangGraph forbids ending on a failing state
Because a human can retype the draft faster than the model

Because convergence means "the loop is done trying," not "the answer is good"; a plain END makes a gave-up draft indistinguishable from a shipped one, so escalation is a distinct terminal state that carries the outcome
Because the counter hasn't been reached yet
questions.explanation: "Done trying" and "good" are different facts; ending unresolved at a bare END makes a failure look like a success, so it ships. A separate escalation terminal records the real outcome. LangGraph allows ending anywhere, human speed isn't the point, and escalation can fire before the counter.

questions.prompt: In the composed router, why must `critique.passed` be checked before the `MAX_REVISIONS` cap?
options: Because the cap is more expensive to evaluate
Because a draft that passes on the final allowed revision is a win, not an escalation; checking the cap first would escalate a good draft
Because `passed` is always true on the last revision
Because the cap can only be read after the critic runs twice
questions.explanation: Success must dominate the cascade: if the cap is tested first, a draft that just passed on the capped pass gets wrongly escalated. Order matters here because both conditions can be true at once. Evaluation cost is irrelevant, `passed` isn't guaranteed on the last pass, and the cap doesn't require two critic runs.

questions.prompt: What is `recursionLimit` in LangGraph, and why is it a backstop rather than your primary control?
options: A prompt field that tells the model how many times to revise
A convergence check that compares two drafts
A billing cap that stops spending when a budget is hit
A per-invocation step cap that throws a GraphRecursionError when exceeded, ending the run with an exception and no outcome, so you set it generously and bound the loop yourself in the router
questions.explanation: recursionLimit throws rather than returning a clean terminal state, which is fine as a safety net but wrong as the real bound; a thrown recursion error in production means your own cap was missing. It isn't a prompt field, a convergence check, or a billing control.

lessons.title: The rubric is the lever, not the prompt

recall.prompt: What does recursionLimit do when a LangGraph invocation exceeds it, and why is that different from your own patterns stopping the loop?
recall.answer: It throws a GraphRecursionError instead of looping forever. Your patterns end in a clean terminal state that carries an outcome downstream code can read, while recursionLimit ends with an exception and a stack trace, no outcome and no graceful escalation.

recall.prompt: How should you set recursionLimit relative to your own termination patterns, and why?
recall.answer: Set it generously, above the worst case your own patterns allow, so in a healthy system it never fires. When it does fire, that is a signal a real bound is missing in your router, not the system working as designed.

recall.prompt: In a reflection loop, what is the highest-impact thing you can edit to improve output quality?
recall.answer: The rubric the critic scores against, not the graph structure or the generation prompt. Termination keeps the loop safe, but the rubric is what makes it good.

recall.prompt: Why keep the rubric as data instead of writing it as prose inside the critique prompt?
recall.answer: Data can be versioned, weighted, linted, and reused as an offline eval. A rubric written as prose in the prompt scores inconsistently from run to run and can't be reused.

recall.prompt: What makes a rubric criterion vague, and why does that hurt the loop?
recall.answer: A criterion is vague when two careful readers could reasonably disagree on pass or fail for the same draft. It makes critique.passed partly random, which causes false passes, wasted revisions, and feedback the writer can't act on.

recall.prompt: How do you turn a vague criterion into a checkable one?
recall.answer: Rewrite it to name something observable in the draft, so you could highlight the words that make it pass. For example, replace 'demonstrates empathy' with 'acknowledges the specific problem the customer reported'.

recall.prompt: What is an overlapping-criteria defect and what two problems does it cause?
recall.answer: Two or more criteria test the same underlying property, so one defect is counted several times. It silently distorts the weighted score and triples the feedback for a single fault.

recall.prompt: What is the test for whether two criteria are independent?
recall.answer: Ask whether you can imagine a draft that passes one and fails the other, and vice versa. If two criteria always pass together and fail together, they're really one criterion, so merge them.

recall.prompt: Why is a compound criterion, one key testing several things, a problem for a reflection loop?
recall.answer: Its single pass or fail can't say which part failed, so the writer's revision is a guess. It costs the loop localization, partial credit, and the ability to weight the parts separately.

recall.prompt: What does findCompoundCriteria detect, and what are its limits?
recall.answer: It flags any description containing the word 'and' or 'or' or a comma, catching the common compound case cheaply. It's a smell detector, not a proof, and it can't catch vague or overlapping criteria, which need human judgment.

lessons.title: Weights and pass rules as data

recall.prompt: Why isn't a bare boolean pass or fail enough from an LLM judge?
recall.answer: You can't tell whether the judge actually read the draft, so you can't trust the verdict, and the writer gets a false with no idea what to change, so the loop can't improve. A verdict needs grounding and a fix, not just a label.

recall.prompt: What two fields does the Module 2 verdict schema require, and how does it force them?
recall.answer: Evidence that grounds the verdict in the draft, and a concrete suggestion for how to fix a failure. Both use z.string().min(1), so structured-output validation rejects any verdict that leaves them empty.

questions.prompt: Why is the rubric, not the generation prompt, the highest-leverage thing to edit in a reflection loop?
options: Because the prompt can't be changed after deployment
Because the model ignores the prompt but obeys the rubric

Because the loop's structure (cycle, router, termination) never improves a draft; the critic does, and the critic is only as good as the rubric, so the rubric must be data you can version, weight, lint, and reuse
Because rubrics are cheaper to run than prompts
questions.explanation: Structure decides when to stop, not whether the draft got better; that's the critic's job, and the critic is only as sharp as its rubric, which is why the rubric is kept as editable data. The prompt can be changed, the model doesn't selectively obey, and cost isn't the argument.

questions.prompt: What is the one-question test for a vague criterion?
options: Could you highlight the exact words in the draft that make it pass? If not (for example "is engaging"), it's vague
Does the criterion contain the word "and"?
Does the criterion have a weight assigned?
Would the criterion pass on the first revision?
questions.explanation: Vague means two careful readers could reasonably disagree on pass/fail for the same draft; the fix-test is whether you can point at the words that earn the pass. The "and" check is the compound test, weights are a separate concern, and first-revision behavior says nothing about vagueness.

questions.prompt: Why is a pair of overlapping criteria a real bug even when each one is individually checkable?
options: Because overlapping criteria always contradict each other
Because the linter can't parse them
Because they make the rubric longer to read
Because they covary, so one defect is counted more than once: the shared property gets outsized weight you never chose and the writer gets multiplied feedback on that axis
questions.explanation: The bug lives in the relationship between the two criteria, not in either alone, so it ships unnoticed: a weighted score double-counts the shared property and the writer over-corrects. Overlap doesn't require contradiction, isn't primarily a parsing problem, and isn't about length.

questions.prompt: Overlap and compound criteria differ how, and which one can a linter catch automatically?
options: They're the same bug; neither can be linted

Overlap is two criteria testing one property (over-counts); compound is one criterion testing two properties (under-resolves), and compound can be linted because it has a textual signature (and, or, commas)
Overlap is one criterion with two properties; compound is two criteria; overlap is the one that can be linted
Both are caught by the same `findCompoundCriteria` linter
questions.explanation: Overlap over-counts a shared property across two criteria; compound under-resolves two properties inside one. Only compound has a reliable textual signature, so `findCompoundCriteria` catches the common case, while vague and overlap need human judgment. Option three reverses the definitions and option four over-claims the linter.

questions.prompt: Why must the judge return evidence and a suggestion, not just a pass/fail boolean?
options: Evidence grounds the verdict so you can audit and trust the routing, and the suggestion gives the writer something to act on so the loop can improve; the schema forces both with `z.string().min(1)`
Because booleans are slower to serialize
Because LangGraph rejects boolean state fields
Because the writer ignores everything except the score
questions.explanation: A score-only critic is a coin flip with extra steps: evidence lets you audit why it routed, and the suggestion is what the next draft acts on. Serialization speed isn't the reason, LangGraph accepts booleans, and the writer specifically reads the suggestion.

questions.prompt: Where do the criteria weights and the pass rule belong, and what breaks if they live in the node code?
options: In the model prompt; nothing breaks
In a database row; the loop stops terminating

In data beside the rubric, applied by `applyPassRule`; in the node, the bar can't be versioned or tuned without a code change and gets re-implemented (and desynced) when the rubric is reused as a Module 4 eval
In the router function; the critic stops returning evidence
questions.explanation: Keeping weights and the pass rule as data lets you tune the bar without touching code and lets the offline eval apply the exact same policy; hard-coding them in the node forces a copy that drifts. They don't belong in the prompt, and their location doesn't affect termination or whether the critic returns evidence.

lessons.title: Why an LLM app is undebuggable without tracing

recall.prompt: Why do the weights and pass rule belong in data rather than in the critique node's code?
recall.answer: Keeping them in data means you change which checks block and what threshold passes without editing, re-reading, or re-testing node code. It also lets the runtime rubric double as Module 4's offline eval instead of a second copy that drifts.

recall.prompt: What is the difference between the all-blocking and weighted-threshold pass rules?
recall.answer: all-blocking passes only when every weight-1 check passes. weighted-threshold passes when earned weight over total weight clears a set threshold, so the same verdicts can fail one rule and pass the other.

recall.prompt: Why can't you debug a reflection loop from its final output alone?
recall.answer: The output collapses several distinct failures into one string. An escalated ticket looks identical whether the loop ran out of budget, stalled on one draft, had a drifting critic, or had a judge that silently errored, so the causes are indistinguishable.

recall.prompt: What is a trace, and how does it differ from a log?
recall.answer: A trace is a structured tree of every step a run took, with one span per node and per LLM call, each carrying inputs, outputs, timing, tokens, and errors. A log is a flat stream you grep, while a trace preserves the run's structure so you can read it as a shape.

lessons.title: What a trace tree shows for a cyclic graph

recall.prompt: When does tracingConfig enable tracing, and why require both conditions?
recall.answer: It enables tracing only when LANGSMITH_TRACING is 'true' and an API key is present. A flag with no key can't upload anything, so treating that state as 'on' would be a lie.

recall.prompt: Why must observability fail soft, and where is the same reflex dangerous?
recall.answer: If tracing is a hard dependency, a monitoring outage takes down your product and blocks developers and CI who have no account. The same fail-soft reflex is dangerous in core logic, where a node that swallows errors and returns a fallback can hide a total failure.

recall.prompt: How do you read the revision count off a cyclic loop's trace tree?
recall.answer: Count the critique_reply spans. Two spans mean one revision, and a long stack of them means the loop nearly hit its cap, which is a smell worth investigating.

recall.prompt: Why do atomic criteria keep a trace legible?
recall.answer: Because the judge scores one criterion at a time, each critique_reply span gets a child span per criterion with its own pass or fail and evidence. A failure names the exact criterion, whereas a compound criterion would collapse those spans into one ambiguous verdict.

recall.prompt: What is a wasted iteration, and how does it appear in the trace?
recall.answer: It's a revision that spent a write call and a critique call without raising the score. In the trace it shows as flat or falling passedChecks down the critique_reply spans, a step no higher than the one before it.

recall.prompt: The trace shows a wasted iteration. What three causes could explain it, and how do you tell them apart?
recall.answer: The feedback wasn't actionable, the writer ignored good feedback, or the criterion is genuinely hard. You tell them apart by reading the suggestion and the next draft side by side in the trace, which the outcome alone can't show.

recall.prompt: What is critic drift, and why is it visible only in the trace?
recall.answer: Critic drift is the same draft receiving different scores on different passes, because the judge is a nondeterministic LLM. It hides in aggregate pass rates and shows up only per-run, on the same draft across passes, which only the trace preserves.

recall.prompt: What two levers reduce critic drift?
recall.answer: Lower the judge's temperature toward zero so scoring is as deterministic as the provider allows, and tighten vague criteria into checkable ones. The trace tells you which criterion drifted so you know which one to sharpen.

lessons.title: A worked real bug from production

recall.prompt: Detecting non-convergence is trivial, so where does the real work go?
recall.answer: The detector just checks whether the outcome is 'escalated'. The real work is triage: reading the score trajectory to learn why the run didn't converge, because the fix differs entirely by cause.

recall.prompt: Why is reflexively raising MAX_REVISIONS a bad fix for non-convergence?
recall.answer: It rescues only the case where the score was climbing but ran out of passes. For a flat-trajectory stall it just buys more identical failing passes at full price, so you read the trajectory before bumping the cap.

questions.prompt: Why can't you debug a reflection loop from its output alone?
options: Because the output is encrypted
Because the output collapses four distinct failures (ran out of budget, stall, critic drift, and a masked critic failure) into one indistinguishable string like `escalated`
Because the output is always empty on failure
Because outputs can only be read inside LangSmith
questions.explanation: A wrong answer and a right one are both just strings, and several very different failures all surface as the same terminal word, so you can't tell them apart to fix them. The output isn't encrypted, isn't empty, and can be read anywhere; the problem is that it's ambiguous.

questions.prompt: Why must tracing be fail-soft, and what is the mirror-image trap?
options: Tracing should fail loud; the trap is making it fail soft
Tracing must be fail-soft; the trap is that it slows the model down
Tracing must be fail-soft; the trap is that traces cost money

Tracing must be fail-soft so a missing key or LangSmith outage never breaks a running loop; the trap is applying that same swallow-the-error reflex to your core nodes, where it hides real bugs. Fail soft on observability, fail loud on the work
questions.explanation: Observability can't be a hard dependency, so tracing degrades quietly; but the same instinct on a work node turns a swallowed error into clean-looking output, which is the masked-failure bug. The other options either invert the rule or name cost and latency, which aren't the trap.

questions.prompt: In a cyclic trace, how do you read the revision count and the score trajectory?
options: Revision count is the number of `critique_reply` spans; the score trajectory is the `passedChecks` read down those spans (for example 2/4 then 4/4 is improving)
Revision count is the total token usage; the trajectory is the latency per span
Revision count is the number of nodes in the graph; the trajectory is the temperature
You can't read either from a trace; you need the database
questions.explanation: Each pass through the critic is one `critique_reply` span, so counting them gives revisions, and reading `passedChecks` down the spans shows whether the draft is climbing. Token usage, latency, node count, and temperature aren't the revision count or trajectory, and the trace does carry this.

questions.prompt: What is critic drift, and why is it invisible in aggregate metrics?
options: The critic getting slower over time; visible as rising latency
The critic running out of budget; visible as truncated output

The same draft scored differently on different passes (the judge isn't a function); invisible in aggregate because average pass rates look fine, so drift only shows per-run on the same draft, which only the trace preserves
The rubric changing between deployments; visible in version control
questions.explanation: Drift is a nondeterministic judge flip-flopping on identical input; averages wash it out, so you only catch it by seeing the same draft score differently within one traced run. The levers are temperature 0 on the judge and tightening the vague criteria where drift concentrates. It isn't latency, truncation, or a rubric edit.

questions.prompt: A run ends `escalated`. Why is that one word three different bugs, and how do you triage it?
options: It's always the same bug: raise `MAX_REVISIONS`
It means the API key is missing; refund the account
It means the rubric is too easy; make it harder

It hides climbing-but-capped (raise the cap), flat (fix the writer or rubric, since raising the cap just buys identical failing passes), and all-zeros (an upstream break); you triage by reading the score trajectory in the trace
questions.explanation: The three causes need opposite fixes, so you can't act until the trajectory tells you which one you have: climbing means you ran out of room, flat means more passes won't help, all-zeros means something upstream broke. Treating it as always one bug, an API key, or an easy rubric misdiagnoses two of the three cases.

questions.prompt: In the worked witus-triage bug, tests stayed green while every classification came back `other` with confidence 0. What was really wrong, and how did the trace reveal it?
options: The rubric was too strict; the trace showed a low pass rate

A fail-soft `classify` node caught every LLM call's error and returned a valid-looking fallback; the real cause was an unfunded API key, and the trace revealed it because the model-call span still carried the actual error ("credit balance too low") that the fallback had erased from the output
The dataset was mislabeled; the trace showed the labels
The graph had a cycle; the trace showed infinite spans
questions.explanation: The output shape stayed valid, so output-only tests passed while the system was fully broken; only the model-call span preserved the real error the fail-soft fallback swallowed. It also poisoned the eval into a fake 8%, the bridge to Module 4. It wasn't a strict rubric, mislabeled data, or a runaway cycle.

lessons.title: Rubric reuse: the runtime critic is the offline test

recall.prompt: How did a fail-soft classify node make a totally broken agent look healthy?
recall.answer: On every LLM error it returned a valid-looking fallback of category 'other' and confidence zero, so output assertions passed and the tests stayed green. The real error was swallowed before it ever reached the output.

recall.prompt: The output said confidence zero. What did the trace show, and why could only the trace show it?
recall.answer: The model-call span carried the actual swallowed error: the API credit balance was too low, an unfunded key rejected for billing. Only the trace could show it because it records the model-call span including its error, before the fallback rewrites the result and erases the evidence.

recall.prompt: Why reuse the runtime critic's rubric as the offline eval instead of writing a separate scorer?
recall.answer: Because the eval then measures exactly what the loop optimizes, so its pass rate is the loop's real success rate. A separate scorer drifts from the runtime standard, and once it does a passing eval stops meaning a good loop.

recall.prompt: What earlier design decision made rubric reuse possible in this project?
recall.answer: Module 2 stored the weights and the pass rule in data, not hard-coded in the critique node. The offline eval applies that same policy without re-implementing it, so the runtime standard and the test standard can't diverge.

lessons.title: A custom evaluator wrapping an LLM judge

recall.prompt: Why deliberately include a case like the `dead` ticket that the loop cannot pass?
recall.answer: Hard cases are the only ones that carry signal, because they move when you change the writer, rubric, or model. A dataset of only easy cases reports 100% and can't catch a regression or confirm an improvement.

recall.prompt: What two design choices matter more than how many examples a dataset has?
recall.answer: Stable ids, so each result traces back to its input and pairwise eval can join two runs by id. And curated coverage of the failure modes rather than random sampling.

lessons.title: Pairwise eval: single-shot vs the reflection loop

recall.prompt: What two values does the custom evaluator return, and why does the second one matter?
recall.answer: It returns `passed`, the score, and `errored`, whether the score is a valid measurement. The `errored` flag stops an infrastructure failure from being averaged into the metric as if it were a genuine bad score.

recall.prompt: How does the harness stop a broken run from being reported as a real number?
recall.answer: `assertNoInfraErrors` throws whenever any case errored, so the eval aborts loudly instead of reporting a number like 0%. A silent fake number is worse than a crash, because someone will cite it.

lessons.title: Setting thresholds with margin for judge noise

recall.prompt: Why compare two systems pairwise instead of reading two separate pass-rate numbers?
recall.answer: Pairwise runs both systems on each same input and counts wins, which is more informative and steadier when an LLM judge is scoring. Relative judgments on the same input are more reliable than two isolated absolute scores.

recall.prompt: What does a thin pairwise margin between the loop and single-shot tell you to do?
recall.answer: Ship the single pass. If the loop wins only a case or two, you're paying two to five times the LLM calls for a small quality gain, so reflection isn't earning its cost.

lessons.title: Why a small dataset catches regressions

recall.prompt: Why set the CI threshold below the expected pass rate instead of requiring 100%?
recall.answer: An LLM judge is nondeterministic, so the pass rate wobbles from run to run. A 100% gate fails on a good system whenever the judge wobbles, and the team learns to ignore a flaky gate.

recall.prompt: How do you size the margin between the expected pass rate and the threshold?
recall.answer: Measure the noise instead of guessing. Run the eval several times on an unchanged good system, see the spread, and put the threshold a clear step below the bottom of it.

questions.prompt: What does it concretely mean that "the runtime rubric is also the offline test"?
options: The offline test re-implements the rubric in a separate file kept in sync by hand
The offline test uses a different, stricter rubric than the loop
The eval calls the same `scoreAgainstRubric` over the same rubric and `applyPassRule` the loop uses at runtime, so there's one definition of "good" read by both
The runtime loop imports the test file and runs the tests on every request
questions.explanation: Reuse means one source of truth: the offline eval and the runtime critic call the identical scorer and pass rule over the identical rubric, so a passing eval actually certifies the loop. A hand-synced copy, a different rubric, or running tests per request all break that single-definition guarantee.

questions.prompt: Why deliberately include a case the loop cannot pass (the `dead` ticket) in the eval dataset?
options: Hard cases are where the signal lives: they're the examples that move when you change the writer, rubric, or model, so a dataset of only easy cases reports 100% and catches nothing
To make the eval run longer so it's more thorough
Because the tooling requires at least one failing example
To lower the average score so the loop looks cautious
questions.explanation: A dataset that everything passes can neither catch a regression nor confirm an improvement; the hard, movable cases are what give the eval discriminating power. Runtime, tool requirements, and cosmetically lowering the average aren't the reason.

questions.prompt: The custom evaluator returns two things: `passed` and `errored`. Why the second?
options: `errored` measures how long the evaluation took

`errored` says whether the score is a valid measurement at all; it detects the error-fallback signature so an infrastructure failure can't be averaged into the metric, preventing the witus-triage "fake 8%" bug
`errored` is a retry counter for flaky network calls
`errored` records which model produced the draft
questions.explanation: `passed` is the score and `errored` is whether that score means anything; without it, a poisoned run gets averaged in as if it were a real low score. It isn't a timer, a retry counter, or a model tag.

questions.prompt: Why compare single-shot and the reflection loop with a pairwise eval instead of two separate pass rates?
options: Because pairwise needs only one model call total
Because two pass rates are impossible to compute
Because pairwise hides where the systems differ, which keeps the report simple

Because judging both systems on the same input and counting wins is more informative (it shows where they differ) and more robust to judge noise, and it reveals when the loop's margin over single-shot is too thin to justify its cost
questions.explanation: Pairwise is the MT-Bench and Arena method: same input, count wins, which is steadier under a noisy judge and shows the margin that justifies the loop's extra cost. It doesn't use fewer calls, two pass rates are computable, and pairwise reveals rather than hides where they differ.

questions.prompt: Why set the eval's passing threshold below the expected pass rate, and what's the common mistake?
options: Because an LLM judge is noisy, so the pass rate wobbles run to run and the gate needs margin to absorb it; the common mistake is a 100% (or too-high) gate that fails on good code whenever the judge wobbles, so the team learns to ignore it
Because a lower threshold makes the eval run faster
Because thresholds must always be round numbers
Because the model performs worse in CI than in production
questions.explanation: Judge noise means the score varies even on identical code, so the gate needs headroom; a gate set at 100% flaps red on good changes until nobody trusts it, and a gate no one trusts catches nothing. Speed, round numbers, and CI-versus-prod aren't the reason.

questions.prompt: What does a roughly 10-example regression dataset buy you, and what does it not?
options: It buys a precise absolute quality number; it doesn't catch regressions
It buys coverage of every rare input; it doesn't run in CI

It buys catching real regressions (which are cliffs across many examples) via curation over volume, cheap enough to run on every commit; it does not buy a precise absolute quality number or coverage of rare inputs, which you catch in production and add later
It buys nothing; only large datasets are useful
questions.explanation: Real regressions are broad cliffs a curated handful will surface, and a tiny set is cheap enough to gate every commit; what it can't give is a precise absolute score (about plus or minus 10%) or rare-input coverage, which the dataset grows to include from production misses. The other options misstate the trade-off.

recall.prompt: Why can a small ten-example dataset catch regressions in a reflection loop?
recall.answer: A regression eval asks a differential question, did this get worse, which needs few examples. Real regressions tank many examples at once so ten curated cases show the cliff, and the eval is cheap enough to run on every commit.

recall.prompt: What does a small dataset not buy you?
recall.answer: It won't give a precise quality number, only about 10% resolution, and it won't cover rare inputs like a one in 500 failure. Those you catch in production and add to the dataset so it grows along the failures it missed.

recall.prompt: What does a reflection loop's convergence rate measure, and where does the data come from?
recall.answer: It's the fraction of live runs that resolved (passed the rubric) rather than escalated. You compute it from the run records the loop already produces, so it needs no new instrumentation.

recall.prompt: How do offline and online evals divide the work, and what keeps them consistent?
recall.answer: The offline eval gates deploys against a fixed dataset in CI. The online eval watches production continuously on live traffic. They share one rubric, so 'healthy' means the same thing in both places.

recall.prompt: What makes a run a runaway, and why is it the most expensive failure?
recall.answer: A runaway run escalated and hit the hard revision cap, so it burned the full budget and produced nothing good. It pays maximum cost for zero output, which is the worst possible cost per good result.

recall.prompt: Why do all three alerts fire on a fully runaway loop?
recall.answer: Zero convergence means no resolved runs, so cost per converged output becomes Infinity and trips the cost alert. That fires alongside the low-convergence and runaway alerts, so one pathology sets off three alarms.

recall.prompt: Why is the critic model such a high-impact cost decision in a reflection loop?
recall.answer: The critic runs once per criterion per revision, far more often than the writer. A four-criterion rubric with two revisions makes about eight critic calls, so the critic dominates both the loop's cost and its quality.

recall.prompt: After an A/B shows a critic wins on quality, what actually decides whether to upgrade?
recall.answer: Quality per cost, not quality alone. Upgrade on a big quality win for modest cost, keep the cheaper critic on a tiny win with a large cost increase, and always take the cheaper critic when quality ties.

recall.prompt: How does runCost price a single run, and what two things drive the cost?
recall.answer: It multiplies the revision count by the cost of one write call plus one critique call per criterion. Cost scales with both the number of revisions and the size of the rubric.

recall.prompt: Why measure cost per converged output instead of average cost per run?
recall.answer: Average cost per run spreads spending across all runs and hides money burned on escalations. Cost per converged output divides total cost by resolved runs only, so failed runs count against the good outputs you shipped, and zero convergence reads Infinity.

lessons.title: Cost and quality: the Pareto frontier

recall.prompt: What does the single-pass good-enough check decide, and how?
recall.answer: It decides whether to run the reflection loop at all. You run the single-shot baseline on your dataset, and if its pass rate already meets the threshold, you ship single-shot and skip the loop.

recall.prompt: Why re-run the single-pass check over time instead of trusting last year's answer?
recall.answer: As models improve, single-shot quality rises, so a loop that was justified before may add cost for no gain now. The answer drifts, so you re-measure rather than assume.

lessons.title: Module 5 quiz: keeping the loop honest

questions.prompt: Convergence rate is the first production metric. What does it tell you, and what does it not?
options: It tells you the model's temperature; it doesn't tell you the cost

It measures the loop's actual job (the fraction of runs that resolved) and moves when reality shifts, but it does not tell you why any single run escalated; the rate finds the when, the trace finds the why
It tells you exactly why each run failed; it doesn't need traces
It measures token cost per run; it doesn't measure quality
questions.explanation: Convergence rate is the loop's success rate over live traffic, comparable over time, and it tells you when to go open the traces; it can't explain a single escalation, which is what Module 3's traces are for. It isn't temperature, a per-run cause, or a cost metric.

questions.prompt: What is a runaway loop, and why is it the most expensive failure?
options: A loop that converges on the first pass; expensive because it under-uses the budget
A loop with a syntax error; expensive because it crashes the server
A run that escalated and hit the hard cap (`escalated && revisions >= maxRevisions`): it burned the full revision budget and resolved nothing, so it pays maximum cost for zero good output
A loop that skips the critic; expensive because quality drops
questions.explanation: A runaway spends every allowed revision and still fails, which is the worst cost-to-value ratio: full price, no result. A first-pass win is the cheapest good case, a crash is a different problem, and skipping the critic isn't what "runaway" means.

questions.prompt: Why does the critic model, more than any other knob, drive both a loop's cost and its quality?
options: Because the critic runs far more often than the writer (one critique call per criterion per revision, for example 8 critic calls to 2 writer calls), so its choice dominates the bill and the convergence; you A/B it on the same dataset and rubric
Because the critic always uses a larger model than the writer
Because the writer is never called after the first pass
Because the critic model sets the recursion limit
questions.explanation: Call volume is the reason: per-criterion, per-revision critique calls vastly outnumber writer calls, so the critic choice moves both cost and convergence more than anything else, and you compare candidates on a fixed dataset and rubric. It needn't be larger, the writer runs every revision, and it doesn't set the recursion limit.

questions.prompt: Why measure cost-per-converged-output instead of average cost per run?
options: Because average cost per run is impossible to compute
Because converged outputs are always cheaper than failed ones
Because per-run cost ignores token prices

Because average-per-run spreads cost over all runs including failures, so a loop that escalates half its runs looks half as expensive as it is; dividing total cost by resolved runs makes money spent on failures visible, and zero convergence gives Infinity, which is honest
questions.explanation: You want the price of a result you can actually use, so you divide by resolved runs; averaging over all runs hides the spend on failures and understates true cost. Average-per-run is computable and does use token prices, and failed runs are often the expensive ones, so the other options are wrong.

questions.prompt: What is the single-pass-good-enough check, and why re-run it over time?
options: It checks whether the loop passes on one try; you re-run it because traffic changes

Before looping, you measure single-shot quality on a dataset; if it clears the bar with margin, ship single-shot and skip the loop (the cheapest reflection is none), and you re-run it because as models improve, single-shot quality rises, so a loop justified last year may not be this year
It's a check that the loop never exceeds one revision; you re-run it for compliance
It's a unit test for the router; you re-run it on every deploy for safety
questions.explanation: The cheapest reflection loop is the one you don't run, so you first test whether a single pass already clears the bar with margin; and because base models keep improving, that answer changes over time, so the check is repeated. It's not about the loop passing in one try, capping revisions, or router unit-testing.

questions.prompt: Define a dominated config and the Pareto frontier, and say whose decision it is where to sit.
options: A dominated config is the cheapest one; the frontier is the most expensive; the engineer picks by taste
A dominated config is any loop with a cap above 1; the frontier is cap-1; marketing decides

A config is dominated when another is at least as good in quality and at least as cheap (strictly better on one axis), so it's never worth running; the Pareto frontier is the set of non-dominated configs; where on it to sit is a product decision, and the engineer's job is to compute the frontier so that decision is made with the trade-off visible
A dominated config is one that crashes; the frontier is the set that never crashes; the on-call engineer decides
questions.explanation: Domination means another config matches or beats it on both axes, so it's off the menu; the frontier is what's left, the real set of trade-offs, and choosing a point on it is a product call the engineer informs by computing it. The other options misdefine domination and misassign the decision.

lessons.section: Module 6 · Capstone on a New Domain
lessons.title: The capstone: composing the whole stack

recall.prompt: When is a configuration dominated, and why should you drop it?
recall.answer: A config is dominated when another is at least as good in quality and at least as cheap, and strictly better on one axis. Dominated configs are never worth running because something beats them on both cost and quality.

recall.prompt: In the worked example, why is loop-cap-3 dominated by loop-cap-1?
recall.answer: Both reach 0.9 quality, but cap-3 costs double. Raising the revision cap from 1 to 3 bought no extra quality and doubled the cost, so cap-3 is strictly wasteful and falls off the frontier.

lessons.section: Module 6 · Capstone on a New Domain
lessons.title: Designing a rubric for a new domain

recall.prompt: What single belief does the capstone put to the test?
recall.answer: That you can redeploy the reflection-loop pattern without the notes, on a domain you have never seen. It composes the whole stack on legal-clause plain-language rewriting, a task unrelated to the support replies it was built on.

recall.prompt: In the capstone, what gets reused from earlier modules and what do you write fresh for the new domain?
recall.answer: Everything structural is reused unchanged: the router, scorer, eval helpers, and metrics all import from support-reply-loop. You write only domain data, meaning a new rubric, a new corpus, and offline stand-ins.

lessons.section: Module 6 · Capstone on a New Domain
lessons.title: Composing the capstone graph

recall.prompt: What is a rubric's 'central tension', and how do you encode it for plain-language rewriting?
recall.answer: It is the pair of goals that pull against each other and define the hard part of the task. For plain language it is fidelity versus simplicity, so preserves_obligation and plain_language are both made blocking, forcing a good rewrite to satisfy both at once.

recall.prompt: Why is direct_address weighted 0.5 instead of 1 in the plain-language rubric?
recall.answer: It is a nudge, not a correctness check. Preferring 'you' is good practice, but a rewrite that is faithful, short, and legalese-free should not fail just for using the third person, so only the three defining criteria block.

lessons.section: Module 6 · Capstone on a New Domain
lessons.title: The capstone eval and dashboard

recall.prompt: How much new graph code does the capstone's plain-language loop actually add?
recall.answer: Almost none. It reuses Module 1's state and router and Module 2's scorer, adding only two thin nodes that call the injected writer and the reused scorer. The one domain-specific change is passing plainLanguageRubric instead of the support rubric.

recall.prompt: Why does routeWithAllPatterns work in the capstone with no changes?
recall.answer: Because it reads only critique.passed, revisionNumber, and the draft history, none of which is domain-specific. So the capstone inherits the hard cap, convergence detection, and the escalation terminal for free.

lessons.section: Module 6 · Capstone on a New Domain
lessons.title: The transfer test: a third domain

recall.prompt: What eval results prove the capstone loop beats single-shot on the legal-clause corpus?
recall.answer: The loop converts 11 of 12 clauses while single-shot converts none, so pairwise gives the loop the win and it clears the margin-aware threshold. The legalese-saturated remittance clause is the one honest miss.

recall.prompt: Module 5's dashboard is reused unchanged, so what single domain input does computeMetrics still need?
recall.answer: The criteria count, passed as plainLanguageRubric.length. Cost scales with rubric size, so that is the only domain fact the cost model needs; everything else reads generic run records.

lessons.section: Module 6 · Capstone on a New Domain
lessons.title: Course close: the model you installed

recall.prompt: How does the transfer test show the pattern carries to a third domain with no scaffolding?
recall.answer: It writes a fresh three-criterion commit-message rubric and a judge, then scores drafts with the same scoreAgainstRubric, RubricCriterion, judge contract, and applyPassRule. A good commit passes and a bad one fails immediately, so the engine never cared what domain it scored.

recall.prompt: What is the course's real exit criterion, beyond the code in the transfer test?
recall.answer: Whether you can do it yourself off the page. Pick a fourth domain the course never named, write its rubric, build the loop from the reused parts, and run the eval and dashboard. Shipping that loop is the proof you hold the pattern as a tool.

lessons.section: Module 6 · Capstone on a New Domain
lessons.title: Module 6 quiz: the whole stack on a new domain

questions.prompt: Why is a brand-new domain essential to the capstone's claim?
options: Because the support-reply code stops compiling on legal text
Because a new domain is easier to score than the old one
Because the tooling needs a second project to store the runs
Because testing on the learned domain proves only recipe-following; transfer to an unseen domain is the real test of a durable mental model
questions.explanation: The capstone's belief is that you can redeploy the pattern without the notes, and only an unseen domain can test that; re-running on the trained domain would just show you can follow the recipe. The code doesn't break on legal text, the new domain isn't easier, and it's not about a second project.

questions.prompt: What central tension does the plain-language rubric encode, and how?
options: Fidelity versus simplicity: making a clause readable tempts you to drop its hard parts and destroy its meaning, so the rubric makes both `preserves_obligation` and `plain_language` blocking, and a good rewrite must satisfy both at once
Speed versus cost: the rubric caps the number of revisions
Length versus tone: the rubric rewards short, friendly clauses
Accuracy versus creativity: the rubric rewards novel phrasing
questions.explanation: Plain language and preserved obligation pull against each other, so the rubric makes both blocking to force a rewrite that's readable without losing the legal meaning. It isn't about speed and cost caps, length and tone, or rewarding creativity.

questions.prompt: Why does the bounded router need zero changes for the new legal-to-plain-language domain?
options: Because the router is rewritten for each domain at deploy time
Because legal text happens to converge faster

Because Module 1 built it generic: `routeWithAllPatterns` reads only `critique.passed`, `revisionNumber`, and the draft history, none of which is domain-specific, so the cap, convergence, and escalation all carry over unchanged
Because the new domain uses no critic
questions.explanation: The router only ever looked at pass/fail, the revision count, and the history, so nothing in it knows or cares about the domain and it transfers with no new code. It isn't rewritten per domain, the transfer doesn't depend on convergence speed, and the new domain still uses a critic.

questions.prompt: The `remittance` clause fails the capstone rubric. Why is that the rubric working correctly?
options: It fails because the corpus is mislabeled, which the eval ignores

It's legalese-saturated, so a naive rewrite keeps a legalese term like "pursuant": it passes fidelity but fails `no_legalese`, and a rubric that discriminates this hard case from a genuinely plain rewrite is exactly what a useful critic must do, so the test asserts it fails
It fails because it's too short to score
It fails at random because the judge is noisy
questions.explanation: A critic that can't tell a sneaky-legalese rewrite from a truly plain one is useless, so the rubric correctly failing the hard `remittance` case is the point, and the test asserts that failure. It's not a labeling error, a length problem, or judge noise.

questions.prompt: What does the third-domain (commit-message) transfer test prove?
options: That commit messages are easier to score than legal clauses
That the loop only works on three specific domains
That you must rewrite `scoreAgainstRubric` for each new domain

That the rubric craft, scorer, and pass rule carry to a domain in neither the course nor the capstone: a fresh three-criterion rubric plugs into the same `scoreAgainstRubric` and splits good commits from bad, so the machinery never knew what domain it scored and the pattern is durable, not domain-shaped
questions.explanation: Carrying the exact same scorer to a third, never-seen domain and having it still separate good from bad is the proof that the pattern is generic, not tuned to support replies or legal text. It's not about relative difficulty, a three-domain limit, or rewriting the scorer.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly assembles the six module beliefs into one description of a reflection loop?
options: A reflection loop is a cyclic graph (M0) with a guaranteed exit (M1), steered by a data rubric an LLM can score (M2), made legible by tracing (M3), protected by an eval that reuses the rubric (M4), and allocated as a budget on a cost and quality curve (M5); because every piece is generic, it transfers
A reflection loop is a linear chain that never stops, steered by a prompt, debugged by print statements, and priced per token
A reflection loop is a single model call with a longer prompt, judged by the same call, capped by the model, and always cheaper than one pass
A reflection loop is a database of drafts, sorted by length, reviewed by a human, and shipped without measurement
questions.explanation: The course's closing summary names each module's contribution in order: graph, guaranteed exit, data rubric, tracing, rubric-reusing eval, and budget allocation, all generic enough to transfer. The other options invert nearly every belief the course installed.
